concepts.title: Molecular Geometry
concepts.conceptBody:
Shapes of Molecules

The shapes or geometry of a molecule is quite accurately predicted by VSEPR ( valence shell electron-pair repulsion ) theory. According to this theory all valence shell electron pairs surrounding the central atom arrange themselves in such a manner as to be as far away from each other as possible. By separating the electrons from each other, electrostatic repulsion, (the cause of higher energy) is minimised. As a result each molecules tends to acquire a state of lowest energy.

The basic ideas can be summarized as follows:

Valence shell pairs of electrons are arranged about the central atom so that repulsions among them are minimized, or so that there is maximum separation among the regions of high electron density (bond pairs) about the atom. For instance, two regions of high electron density would be most stable on opposite sides of the central atom (the linear arrangement), while three regions would be most stable when they are arranged at the corners of an equilateral triangle (the trigonal planar arrangement). The resulting arrangement of these regions is referred to as the electronic geometry of the central atom.

We will discuss this table detail a little later.

Some important points:

(1) a covalent bond is formed by overlapping of atomic orbitals of a valency shell of the two atoms. As a result of overlapping, there is maximum electron density some where between the two atoms.

(2) Greater the overlapping, higher is the strength of chemical bond.

(3) Electrons which are already paired in valency shell can enter into bond formation if they can be unpaired first and shifted to vacant orbitals of slightly higher energy of the same main energy shell. (Valency shell).

This point explains the trivalency of boron, tetravalency of carbon, pentavalency of phosphorus, hexavalency of sulphur inspite of the fact that these atoms have paired orbitals in the valency shell.

(4) Between 2 orbitals of same stability (i.e. having same energy) one more directionally concentrated would form a stronger bond. Dumb-bell shaped p-orbitals will form a stronger bond as compared to spherically symmetrical s-orbital. It is formed by head on or axial overlap.

The types of bonds can be formed an account of overlapping.

(1) Sigma Bond: A bond formed between two atoms by the overlap of singly occupied orbitals along their axes (end to end overlap) is called sigma ($\sigma$) bond. The bond formed by this type of over lapping is very strong as the extent of overlapping is sufficiently high. In sigma bond, the electron density accumulates between the centres of the atoms being bounded and lies on the imaginary line joining the nuclei of bonded atoms.

Sigma bonds are formed by three types of overlapping.

(i) s-s overlapping: 

For example, hydrogen molecule. 

Each hydrogen atom has one electron in is orbital which is spherical. Is orbital of both the hydrogen atoms approach each other closely and when they reach a point of maximum attraction by the two nuclei, they overlap and form a sigma bond.

Formation of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ molecule by s-s overlapping

The bond has two electrons which have opposite spins. The probability of finding these electrons is maximum in the region between the two nuceli on the molecular axis. The electron density of the bond is distributed symmetrically about the molecular axis.

(ii) s-p overapping (Formation of $\mathbf{HF,}\,\,{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{O}$ molecules); 

(a) In the formation of $\text{HF}$ molecule the 1s-orbital of hydrogen overlaps with the p-orbital of fluorine containing unpaired electron.

(b) Formation of water molecule: Oxygen atom has the configuration of valency shell i.e., it has two orbitals singly occupied. These two orbitals overlap with $1~\text{s}$ orbital of two hydrogen atoms forming sigma bonds.

Formation of water molecule by ${{\text{s}}^{-}}\text{p}$ overlapping

Since the two orbitals of oxygen are at right angle to each other an angle of 90 is expected between two sigma bonds but actual bond angle observed is 104.5 .

(iii) p-p overlapping (Formation of fluorine molecule): This is illustrated by the formation of fluorine molecule. The electronic configuration of fluorine atom is $1~{{\text{s}}^{2}}2~{{\text{s}}^{2}}2\text{p}_{\text{x}}^{2}2\text{p}_{\text{y}}^{2}2\text{p}_{\text{z}}^{1}$, i.e., one orbital is singly occupied. When p-orbitals of two fluorine atoms approach each other with their heads directly towards one another, they overlap and form a sigma bond.

$\text{ Formation of }{{\text{F}}_{2}}\text{ molecule by p-p overlapping }$

(2) Pi $(\pi )$ Bond 

$\pi$-bonds are formed by the sidewise or lateral overlapping of p-orbitals. The overlapping takes place at the side of two lobes and hence, the extent of overlapping is relatively smaller. Thus, $\pi $-bond is a weaker bond in comparison to sigma bond. The molecular orbital is oriented above and below the plane containing nuclear axis.

Formation of $\pi $-bond

Formation of oxygen molecule: Oxygen atom has two p-orbitals singly occupied in the valency shell. When two oxygen atoms approach each other, one set of p-orbitals experiences head on overlap forming a sigma bond while other set of p-orbitals overlaps sidewise to form a $\pi $-bond. Thus, oxygen molecule has one $\sigma $-and one $\pi $-bond.

$\text{ Formation of }{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{ molecule }$

Similarly, the formation of nitrogen molecule can be explained. It has one sigma bond and two $\pi $-bonds.

All single bonds are sigma bonds. A double bond consists of one sigma and one $\pi $-one while a triple bond consists of one sigma and two $\pi $-bonds.

(i) Bond energy increases from a single bond to a trip bond.

Bond energy: Single bond < Double bond < Triple bond

(ii) Bond strength increases from a single bond to a triple bond.

Bond strength: Single bond < Double bond < Triple bond

(iii) Bond length of a multiple bond (double or triple) is always shorter than the corresponding single bond.

Bond length: Single bond > Double bond > Triple bond

(iv) Reactivity of a multiple bond is always more than the single bond. This is due to the fact that \[\pi \]-electrons are mobile in nature.

Reactivity: Single bond < Double bond < Triple bond.

concepts.title: Mass-Mass Relationship
concepts.conceptBody:
Mass-Mass Relationship

This relates the mass of a species (reactant or product) with the mass of another species (reactant or product) involved in a chemical reaction.

Let us consider a chemical reaction

$\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}(~\text{s})\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{CaO}(\text{s})+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})$

Let the mass of $\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ taken be $\text{xg}$ and we want to calculate the mass of CaO obtained by heating $\text{xgCaCO}$. Then the moles of $\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ taken would be $\frac{\text{x}}{{{\text{M}}_{1}}}$ (where ${{\text{M}}_{1}}$ represents the molar mass of $\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ ). According to the balanced reaction, the molar ratio of $\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ and $\text{CaO}$ is 1: 1. So same number of moles $\left( \frac{\text{x}}{{{\text{M}}_{1}}} \right)$ of $\text{CaO}$ would be formed. Now for converting the moles of $\text{CaO}$ into mass of $\text{CaO}$ obtained, we need to multiply the moles of $\text{CaO}$ with the molar mass of CaO. Let the molar mass of CaO be ${{\text{M}}_{2}}$, so the mass of $\text{CaO}$ obtained by heating $\text{x}g$ of $\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ would be $\left( \frac{\text{x}}{{{\text{M}}_{1}}}\times {{\text{M}}_{2}} \right)\text{g}$.

Example 3

What will be the mass of CaO produced by 10 gm of $\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$. The reaction is

$\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}(~\text{s})\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{CaO}(\text{s})+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})$

Solution. 

\(\begin{array}{ccccc} & \mathrm{CaCO}_3(\mathrm{~s})^{-} \rightarrow & \mathrm{CaO}(\mathrm{s}) 1 & \mathrm{CO}_2 \mathrm{I} \\ \text { At } t=0 & 10 \mathrm{gm} & 0 & 0 \\ \text { At } t=\infty & \begin{array}{l} 0 \text { (completely } \\ \text { consumed) } \end{array} & ? & \text { ? } \\ \end{array}\)

No. of moles of $\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\text{ }=\frac{\text{ wt}\text{. in gm }}{\text{ Mol}\text{. wt }}=\frac{10}{100}=0.1\text{ mole }$

from mole concept

$1\text{ mole of CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}=1\text{ mole of CaO}$

$\Rightarrow $ moles of $\text{CaO}$ produced will be equal to moles of $\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$

\[\text{ moles of CaO}(\text{s})\text{ }=0.1\text{ mole}\]

$\text{ moles of CaO}(\text{s})\text{ }=0.1\text{ mole wt}\text{. of CaO }$

\[\text{wt}\text{. of CaO }=0.1\text{ mol}\text{. wt}\text{. of CaO}\]

\[\text{ }=0.156=5.6\text{gm}\]

concepts.title: Mass-Volume Relationship
concepts.conceptBody:
Mass-Volume Relationship

This establishes the relationship between the mass of a species (reactant or product) and the volume of a gaseous species (reactant or product) involved in a chemical reaction. Let us take$\text{x}g$ of $\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ in a vessel of capacity $\text{VL}$ and the vessel is heated so that $\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ decomposes as

$\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}(~\text{s})\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{CaO}(\text{s})+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})$

We want to find out the volume of $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ evolved at STP by heating $\text{x}g$ of $\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$. Then Moles of $\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}=\frac{\text{x}}{{{\text{M}}_{1}}}$ (since molar ratio of $\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ and $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is 1: 1 )

$\therefore \quad $ Volume of $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ evolved at $\text{STP}=\left( \frac{\text{x}}{{{\text{M}}_{1}}}\times 22.4 \right)\text{L}$

But, if the volume of $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ evolved is to be calculated at pressure $\text{P}$ and temperature $\text{TK}$. Then, moles of $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ evolved $=\frac{\text{x}}{{{\text{M}}_{1}}}$

Volume of $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ evolved at pressure $\text{P}$ and temperature $\text{T}=\frac{\text{x}}{{{\text{M}}_{1}}}\times \frac{\text{RT}}{\text{P}}(\text{UsingPV}=\text{nRT})$

Example 4

What volume of oxygen (NTP) will be required to react with $1\text{gm}$ of Ca?

Solution. 

$\text{Ca}(\text{s})+\frac{1}{2}{{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{CaO}(\text{s})$ 

$\text{t}=0$ 1 gm?

Applying mole concept

$1\text{ mole of Ca}=\frac{1}{2}\text{ mole of }{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

Since volume of 1 mole of ${{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})$ at $\text{NTP}=22.4\text{lt}$.

$\therefore \quad $ volume of $\frac{1}{2}$ mole of ${{\text{O}}_{2}}(~\text{g})$ at $\text{NTP}=11.2\text{lt}$.

There can be written as

1 mole of $\text{Ca}$ or $40\text{gm}$ of $\text{Ca}$ react with 11.2 lit. of ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$ at $\text{NTP}$

$\therefore \quad $ $1\,\,\text{gm}\,\,\text{Ca}$ react with $=\frac{11.2}{40}=0.28\text{lt}$.

concepts.title: Volume-Volume Relationship
concepts.conceptBody:
Volume-volume Relationship

This relationship deals with the volume of a gaseous species (reactant or product) with the volume of another gaseous species (reactant or product) involved in a chemical reaction.

Let us consider the reaction, ${{\text{N}}_{2}}(~\text{g})+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}(~\text{g})\to 2\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}(~\text{g})$. We are given $\text{xL}$ of ${{\text{N}}_{2}}$ at pressure $\text{P}$ atm and temperature $\text{TK}$ and we want to know the volume of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ required to react with it at the same pressure and temperature.

Moles of $\text{ }{{\text{N}}_{2}}=\frac{\text{P}\times \text{x}}{\text{RT}}$

Moles of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ required $=\frac{3\times \text{P}\times \text{x}}{\text{RT}}$(since molar ratio of ${{\text{N}}_{2}}\text{ }{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{ is }1:3$)

$\therefore $ Volume of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ required at same pressure temperature $\text{=}\frac{\text{3}\times \text{P}\times \text{x}\times \text{RT}}{\text{RT}\times \text{P}}\text{=3}\times \text{L}$

This result could also have been obtained by knowing that for a gaseous relation, at the same pressure and temperature, the moles of gas is directly proportional to volume of the gas ($\text{V}\alpha \text{n}$ since $\text{P }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ T}$ are constant) or molar ratio and volume ratio are same.

Thus, when $x~\text{L}$ of ${{\text{N}}_{2}}$ is taken at Pressure $\text{P}$ atm temperature $\text{TK}$, then at the same pressure and temperature, the volume of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ required would be $3\times \text{L}$ (since the volume ratio of ${{\text{N}}_{2}}$ & ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ would be same as molar ratio i.e. 1: 3). But if the volume of${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ required is to be calculated at another pressure $\text{P}$ atm and temperature $\text{{T}'}~\text{K}$, then

Moles of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ required $=\frac{3\times \text{P}\times \text{x}}{\text{RT}}$

$\therefore \quad $ Volume of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ required at pressure ${{\text{P}}^{\prime }}$ atm & temperature ${{\text{T}}^{\prime }}\text{K}$

$=\frac{3\times \text{P}\times \text{x}\times \text{R}{{\text{T}}^{\prime }}}{\text{RT}\times {{\text{P}}^{\prime }}}=\left( \frac{3\text{x}\times \text{P}{{\text{T}}^{\prime }}}{{{\text{P}}^{\prime }}\text{T}} \right)\text{L}$

Example 5 

$8\text{gm}$ of methane is burnt with $4.48~\text{L}$ of ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$ at STP. Find out the volume of $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ gas produced at STP and also the weight of $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ gas.

Solution. 

The balanced chemical equation is

$\begin{matrix} {} & C{{H}_{4}} & + & 2{{O}_{2}} & \to & C{{O}_{2}} & + & 2{{H}_{2}}O \\ {} & 1\,\,mol & {} & 2\,\,mol & {} & 1\,\,mol & {} & {} \\ Ideal & 16\,\,gm & {} & 2\,\,22.4\,\,L & {} & 22.4\,\,L & {} & {} \\ {} & {} & {} & {} & {} & 44\,\,gm & {} & {} \\ \end{matrix}$

No. of moles of $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}=\frac{8}{16}=0.5~\text{mol}$

No. of moles of ${{\text{O}}_{2}}=\frac{4.48~\text{L}}{22.4~\text{L}}=0.2~\text{mol}$

Now since 1 mole of $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ required 2 mole (i.e., 44.82) of ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$ for complete combustion. But the given moles of ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is only $0.2~\text{mol}$. So, ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is the limiting reagent.

Again, since 2 moles of ${{O}_{2}}$ reacts with 1 mole of $C{{H}_{4}}$ to give 22.4 L of $C{{O}_{2}}$ at STP.

So 0.2 mole of ${{\text{O}}_{2}}$ will react with $0.1~\text{mol}$ of $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ to give $2.24~\text{L}$ of $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$.

Wt. of $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ produced = 0.1 mol 44

= 4.4 gm of $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

Example 6

$\text{NaCl}$ of $95%$ purity is used to prepare salt cake $\left( \text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}} \right)$ by the reaction,

$2\text{NaCl}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}\to \text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+2\text{HCl}$

If the product $\left( \text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}} \right)$ is only $85%$ pure, what weight of $\text{NaCl}$ is used up in producing $1~\text{kg}$ of the impure salt cake?

Solution. 

Let the weight of $\text{NaCl}$ used up in producing $1~\text{kg}$ of impure product be $\text{x}$.

Mass of pure $\text{NaCl}=\frac{\text{x}\times 95}{100}$

Moles of pure $\text{NaCl}=\frac{\text{x}\times 95}{100\times 58.5}$

Moles of pure $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}=\frac{\text{x}\times 95}{100\times 58.5}\times \frac{1}{2}$

Mass of pure $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}=\frac{\text{x}\times 95}{100\times 58.5}\times \frac{1}{2}\times 142=\frac{100\times 85}{100}$

$\therefore x=73.72~\text{g}$

concepts.title: Molarity,Molality and Normality
concepts.conceptBody:
Concentration terms

Molarity (M): The no. of moles of solute present in one litre of solution is called the molarity (M).

$\text{ Molarity }=\frac{\text{ No}\text{. of moles of solute }}{\text{ Volume of solution in litre }}$

or $\text{ Molarity }=\frac{\text{ Wt}\text{. of solute/Molecular wt}\text{. of solute }}{\text{ Volume of solution in litre }}$

So, $\quad $ no. of moles of solute = Volume of solution (in litre) / Molarity of solution

Molality (m): No. of moles of solute present in one kilogram of solvent is know as molality.

$\text{ Molality }(\text{m})=\frac{\text{ No}\text{. of moles of solute }}{\text{ Wt}\text{. of solvent }(\text{ in kg})}$

Normality (N): No. of equivalents of solute present in one litre of the solution is known as Normality (N).

$\text{Normality }(\text{N})\text{ }=\frac{\text{ No}\text{. of equivalents of solute }}{\text{ Volume of solution in litre }}$

$=\frac{\text{ Wt}\text{. of solute/equivalent weight of solute }}{\text{ Volume of solution in litre }}$

No. of equivalent of solute = Volume of solution (in litre) / normality of solution

No. of milli equivalents of solute = Normality of solution / Volume of solution in millilitres
